coredata.dc:title: Fluorine doping induced fluorescence emission redshift of CDs
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jianen
Guan, Li
Luo, Tao
Yin, Tao
Shi, Weicai
Ren, Xiaojie
Lan, Youshi
Wang, Fenghe
Li, Xu
coredata.dc:description: Elemental doping is an effective way to modify the optical properties of carbon dots (CDs). In this work, the effects of fluorine-doped (F-doped) types and configurations on the structural stability and fluorescence properties of CDs were investigated based on density functional theory and time-dependent density functional theory. Circumcoronene was selected as an undoped structure, and a series of F-doped CD structures including edge and basal plane fluorine-doping (F-doping) types, and CF, CF2 and CF3 configurations were constructed. The calculation results show that the edge CF groups play a more positive role in the structural stability of CDs than the CF2 and basal plane CF groups. Different from the three configurations, the CF3 groups cannot contribute to the structural stability. For optical properties, the edge CF and CF3 groups have less effect on the electron distribution of CDs, hence only a slight fluorescence emission redshift occurs in two F-doping cases. In contrast, the CF2 and basal plane CF groups can evidently influence the highest occupied molecular orbital of the CDs, which induces the charge transfer transition and the fluorescence emission is redshifted to the near-infrared region. This work is helpful to predict the F-doped types and configurations in CDs, reveals the modulation mechanism of F-doping on the fluorescence properties and provides a theoretical guidance for the controlled synthesis of CDs in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorine doping
Structural stability
Fluorescence wavelength redshift

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62075055, No. 62175075, No. 61974009), the Central Project Guide Local Science and Technology for Development of Hebei Province (No. 226Z1103G), and the Interdisciplinary Research Project of Hebei University (No. DXK202012). We also appreciate the High Performance Supercomputer Center of Hebei University.

This work was partly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62075055 , No. 62175075 , No. 61974009 ), the Central Project Guide Local Science and Technology for Development of Hebei Province ( 226Z1103G ), and the Interdisciplinary Research Project of Hebei University (No. DXK202012 ). We also appreciate the High Performance Supercomputer Center of Hebei University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Top and side views of 12 relaxed F-doped structures. Carbon, hydrogen, and fluorine atoms are colored gray, white, and blue, respectively. The label below each graph indicates F-doping types and configurations.
C-F bond lengths (Å) and cohesive energies (eV) for all the F-doped CD structures. The red line is the cohesion energy of circumcoronene.
HOMO-LUMO band gap for the first excited state of circumcoronene and different F-doped structures, and the values in the middle of HOMO and LUMO represent the band gaps.

NTOs of circumcoronene and different F-doped structures (isovalue=0.02). The eigenvalue (λi) of the orbital wave function gives the configuration component of the excited state. The topological surface maps below and above the arrows represent the states where the electrons leave and arrive, respectively. The red circle marks the F-doping sites.

CDD was analyzed for the electronic transition processes from the first excited state S1 to the ground state S0 F-doped structures. (isovalue=0.005) The blue and green color region represent the decrease and increase in charge density of the excited state relative to the ground state, respectively.
Fluorescence emission wavelengths (λ em), oscillator strengths (f), and charge transfer values (q) between F atoms and carbon cores for circumcoronene and 12 F-doped structures.

head.ce:title: Fluorine doping induced fluorescence emission redshift of CDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of High-precision Computation and Application of Quantum Field Theory of Hebei Province, College of Physics Science and Technology, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of High-precision Computation and Application of Quantum Field Theory of Hebei Province
College of Physics Science and Technology, Hebei University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory of High-precision Computation and Application of Quantum Field Theory of Hebei Province, College of Physics Science and Technology, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiochemistry, China Institute of Atomic Energy, Beijing 102413, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Radiochemistry, China Institute of Atomic Energy, Beijing 102413, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Elemental doping is an effective way to modify the optical properties of carbon dots (CDs). In this work, the effects of fluorine-doped (F-doped) types and configurations on the structural stability and fluorescence properties of CDs were investigated based on density functional theory and time-dependent density functional theory. Circumcoronene was selected as an undoped structure, and a series of F-doped CD structures including edge and basal plane fluorine-doping (F-doping) types, and CF, CF2 and CF3 configurations were constructed. The calculation results show that the edge CF groups play a more positive role in the structural stability of CDs than the CF2 and basal plane CF groups. Different from the three configurations, the CF3 groups cannot contribute to the structural stability. For optical properties, the edge CF and CF3 groups have less effect on the electron distribution of CDs, hence only a slight fluorescence emission redshift occurs in two F-doping cases. In contrast, the CF2 and basal plane CF groups can evidently influence the highest occupied molecular orbital of the CDs, which induces the charge transfer transition and the fluorescence emission is redshifted to the near-infrared region. This work is helpful to predict the F-doped types and configurations in CDs, reveals the modulation mechanism of F-doping on the fluorescence properties and provides a theoretical guidance for the controlled synthesis of CDs in the future.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past two decades, carbon dots (CDs) have become a hot topic due to excellent properties [1–3], such as easy preparation [4], good water-solubility [5], good biocompatibility [6], low toxicity [7], etc. Usually, CDs are composed of carbon cores including sp 2 carbon domains and sp 3 hybridized carbons, and carbon shells including surface functional groups and molecular fluorophores [8–10]. The smaller size (less than 10 nm) can induce the quantum confinement effect and prompt the CDs to exhibit photoluminescent properties [11,12]. Therefore, CDs exhibit a diverse range of promising applications [13,14], such as light-emitting diodes [15], biosensing [13], bioimaging [15], solar cells [16], drug delivery [17], photocatalysis [18], and so forth.

The fluorescent properties of CDs are closely related to atomic structures, which can be modified by surface functionalization and heteroatom doping [8,19-21]. Due to atomic orbital overlaps, the introduction of heteroatoms in the synthesis process can significantly change the electronic structures and optical properties of CDs [22–24]. Nowadays, fluorine (F), nitrogen (N), and phosphorus (P) have been commonly used to modulate the fluorescence properties of CDs [25–28]. Among these common heteroatoms, F is one of the most common elements on the earth, and its unique chemical and physical properties have attracted much attention in synthesizing CDs [29,30]. In the Periodic Table, F atom has a very small atomic radius, which will facilitate the doping of F atoms in carbon networks [30,31]. And F has the highest electronegativity 4.0, which makes F atoms exhibit a strong electron-pulling characteristic [32]. It suggests that after F is introduced into the carbon network, the strong C-F bonds along with the hydrogen bonds between F and H atom (F-H···F) will improve the stability of the fluorine-doped (F-doped) CDs [28,33]. In addition, the high affinity of F atoms for cell membranes, significant redshift emission and enhanced quantum efficiency due to fluorine-doping (F-doping) are some advantages that make F-doping CDs suitable for biological applications, as demonstrated by many experimental works [34,35]. And some F-doping CDs synthesized by quinolone antibiotics present effective antibacterial activity, which retained the antibacterial bioactivities features of the precursors, which suggests that such fluorescent CDs can be used as bio-antimicrobial materials [36].

A large amount of experimental work has demonstrated that fluorine doped carbon dots have many outstanding properties. Sun et al. found that the edge C-F bonds provided a protective shell for carbon domains and thus reduced the reactivity of CDs and improved the pH stability of fluorescence [37]. Wang et al. found the F-doped CDs in highly ionic solutions exhibited excellent fluorescence stability over a wide pH range under 24 h photobleaching conditions [28]. Therefore, F-doped CDs are suitable for the applications of long-term cellular imaging [38]. Besides, many experimental works observed that F-doping not only can strengthen the stability of CDs, but also cause a redshift and reach the near-infrared region in the fluorescence spectrum, which is highly beneficial for biological applications, such as near-infrared light can produce a deeper penetration into biological tissues [39]. Recently, Ding et al. synthesized F-doped CDs exhibiting a 676 nm fluorescence emission. Notably, the fluorescence emission wavelength experienced a redshift of 52 nm in dimethylsulfoxide solvent and a remarkable 172 nm redshift in solid CDs when F-doping was performed. Furthermore, this F-doping procedure resulted in a significant enhancement of quantum efficiency, boosting it from 6.57 % to an impressive 14.57 %. By measuring electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (ESR), they revealed the redshift of emission wavelength possibly originated from F atoms bound to the edge or plane of the sp 2 carbon domains [40]. Mo et al. restricted CDs in SiO2 nanoparticles and found that the co-doping of F with N caused a nearly 100 nm fluorescence emission redshift and an increase in fluorescence quantum efficiency from 5.04 % to 58.8 % relative to the case of N single-doped CDs [41]. Cheng et al. synthesized deep-red emissive F-doping CDs who has a maximum emission of 636 nm and an absolute quantum yield of 36 %. The intensity of the F- doping CDs was stable in different concentrations of NaCl solution and remained above 85 % when exposed to daylight for a week. Therefore, the prepared F-doped CDs possessed wonderful optical properties, high quantum yield and good stability [42]. Hua et al. prepared tunable long-wavelength emission F-doping CDs, which exhibit a high fluorescence quantum yield of 52.2 %. F-doping not only improves the quantum efficiency of the CDs but also prevents aggregation-caused quenching [36]. Ding et al. prepared solid-state F-doping CDs with bright red fluorescence. The solid-state fluorescence may be due to the fluorine induced defect traps on the surface/edge of F-doped CDs [40]. Kumari et al. prepared fluorine embedded solid-state CDs exhibiting polymer-induced emission, which is attributed to hydrogen bonds formed between polyvinyl alcohol and F atoms eliminate the π-π interaction of the conjugated core [33].

Although the previously experimental works have shown that F-doping can effectively enhance the stability of CDs, improve quantum efficiency and induce a significant fluorescence emission redshift, theoretical studies on F-doping lag behind experimental researches [14,43-45]. Many theoretical works about this issue have been successfully performed by using density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density-functional theory (TDDFT). The ground state structures and its thermodynamic stability of F-doped carbon dots are suitable to be calculated using DFT, while the excited state structures can be calculated using TDDFT, and then the fluorescence properties and the electron transition behavior during fluorescence emission can be analyzed. Yang et al. speculated that F-doping extended the π-electron systems and thus lead to fluorescence emission redshift, while Wang et al. believed that the redshift was caused by hydrogen bonds formed during F-doping at high concentrations [28,44]. Recently, several theoretical works investigated the structural characteristics of F-doped configurations in CDs and pointed out that there are three typical structures, CF groups, CF2 groups and CF3 groups, in F-doped CDs, and they found that the C-F bond configuration has a significant effect on the band gap and fluorescence properties of CDs [46–50]. In addition, the fluorescence emission wavelength is also influenced by the site where F atoms are doped, whether it is at the edge or basal plane [50]. Obviously, the doping sites and configurations play an important role in the structure and fluorescence properties of F-doped CDs. However, there are still controversies regarding the existing theoretical analyses, and the underlying mechanism of F atoms inducing fluorescence emission redshift still needs to be further investigated to promote the desired optical properties of CDs [44].

In this work, a series of F-doped CD structures were constructed based on circumcoronene as a pristine structure. The structural stability, electronic and optical properties of F-doped CDs with various doped types and configurations were systematically investigated by using DFT and TDDFT. A theoretical prediction for doped types and configurations of F atoms in CDs was described and the fluorescence mechanism was discussed in detail.

The geometric configurations of different F-doped CDs are depicted in Fig. 1 . Here, circumcoronene regarded as an original mode has a hexagonal zigzag-edged polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons structure, consisting of 54 carbon atoms with edges saturated with hydrogen atoms. There are applicability and limitations in using circumcoronene to simulate complex CDs, and detailed discussion are in the Supporting Information. In Fig. 1, three configurations of F bonded with C atoms including CF group, CF2 group and CF3 group, and six doped sites according to the symmetry of circumcoronene were finally constructed. In F-doped CDs, CF groups can be classified into edge and basal plane types, and CF2 and CF3 groups only exhibit the edge type. For simplicity, these 12 modes were named according to the characteristics of F-doped configurations. Taking “edge-CF-1″ and “edge-CF-2″ as examples, the former indicates that one F atom is bonded to one edge C atom at site 1 of circumcoronene, and the latter means F atom is bonded with one edge C atom at site 2. Here, site 1 and site 2 belong to different lattice symmetries, and site 1 has a higher symmetry than site 2.

Subsequently, the geometric configurations of all F-doped CDs are analyzed. For edge-CF-1 and edge-CF-2 structures, the F-doped sites are located at the edge sites of carbon networks. For edge-CF2–1 and edge-CF2–2 structures, two F atoms are simultaneously attached to one edge C atom, which suggests that the electronic structure of the C atom undergoes a transition from sp 2 hybridization state to sp 3 hybridization state. For edge-CF3–1 and edge-CF3–2 structures, three F atoms are simultaneously attached to one additional C atom as a CF3 group at the edge sites of carbon networks. Basal plane CF groups mean that one out-of-plane F atom is covalently bonded to one C atom without creating vacancies on the carbon network. For basal plane-CF-1 and basal plane-CF-2 structures, the hybridization of C atoms is exhibited in sp 3 hybridization state. For the other four basal plane-CF-3, 4, 5, 6 structures, the F atoms are normal to the carbon plane and the numbers from 3 to 6 represent four doped sites from the edge site to the center site. In Fig. 1, one can see that for the basal plane CF groups, the C atoms bonded with F atoms are somewhat pulled out from the carbon networks due to the strong pull electron effect of F atoms.

In the present work, the Gaussian program package [51] was used to calculate the geometrical structures and optical properties of CDs. The geometrical optimization, energy band calculations, frequency analysis and electronic transitions of circumcoronene and 12 F-doped structures were performed using the range separated hybrid functional ωB97X-D with dispersion correction combined with the solvent effect of aqueous solution was taken into account using the polarizable continuum model (PCM) method [52–54]. The geometry was first optimized with the def2-SVP basis set, followed by calculation of energies with the def2-TZVP basis set. All the structures in the ground state have been optimized without any symmetry restriction and completely relaxed until there are no any imaginary frequencies. More computational details are provided in the Supporting Information. To verify the reliability of the present calculation method, the fluorescence emission spectrum of circumcoronene was first investigated. The results show that the fluorescence emission wavelength is 497 nm, corresponding to an energy of 2.49 eV, which is very close to previously theoretical results of 496 nm and 2.46 eV calculated by Geethalakshmi et al. and Shi et al., respectively [55,56]. It suggests that the present method is suitable for describing F-doped CD structures. Here, the electronic properties include natural transition orbitals (NTOs) and charge density difference (CDD) of all structures were analyzed using Multiwfn software [57].

The cohesive energy is one important physical quantity to predict the structural stability of materials from a thermodynamic perspective [58,59]. Usually, if the cohesive energy is negative, it means that the structure is stable, and the smaller value indicates a more stable structure. To investigate the influence of F-doping on the structural stability of CDs, the cohesive energies of all structures were calculated. Just like the following equation, the cohesive energy can be represented as the difference between the total energy of one CD structure and the sum of the energies of individual atoms.
(1)
E coh
=
E CDs
−
(
N C
E C
+
N F
E F
+
N H
E H
)
N C
+
N F
+
N H
where E coh is the cohesive energy of the CD structure, E CDs is the total energy of CD structure, E C, E F and E H represent the energies of individual C, F and H atoms, respectively, and N C, N F and N H denote the numbers of C, F and H atoms, respectively.

Firstly, all the geometric configurations were optimized and then a preliminary analysis of conjugation properties was obtained. As we all know, the conjugation property of CD structures has a close relationship with its fluorescence emission characteristics [60]. For edge-CF-1 and edge-CF-2 structures, one edge C atom was bonded with one F atom and all carbon atoms remained sp 2 hybridized, thus the conjugation properties of the carbon networks have no fundamental change. For edge-CF2–1 and edge-CF2–2 structures, one edge C atom was simultaneously bonded with two F atoms, and therefore the origin sp 2 carbon domain is partially destroyed and the sp 3 hybridization state appears around the F atom. For edge-CF3–1 and edge-CF3–2 structures, a CF3 group is bonded to one edge C atom and the conjugation properties of sp 2 carbon domains remain essentially unchanged, just like the case of the edge CF group. For six basal plane CF structures, the C atoms are bonded with out-of-plane F atoms, which triggers a change in the electronic state from sp 2 to sp 3 hybridized state, suggesting that the origin conjugation systems of the carbon networks have been partially disrupted. Based on the above analysis, one can see that the F-doping of edge CF and CF3 groups has no essential influence on the original conjugation properties of the CDs, whereas the CF2 and basal plane CF group can partially modulate the conjugation systems and then the fluorescence spectra. The relationship between electronic conjugation properties and fluorescence emission will be discussed further in the subsequent section.

The C-F bond length has an important effect on the atomic structure and fluorescence properties of CDs. The formation of chemical bonds is related to the electronegativity of atoms, and the electronegativities of F and C atoms are 4.0 and 2.5, respectively. Depending on the chemical reaction conditions, C-F bonds possibly exist in three types, that is, ionic, semi-ionic, or covalent bonds [32]. With the decreasing concentrations of F atoms in CDs, the relative abundance of C-F bonds varies from covalent bonds to semi-ionic bonds, and finally the C-F bonds become ionic at very low F concentration [50]. The empirical bond lengths of ionic, semi-ionic, and covalent C-F bonds are 3.0 Å, 1.7 Å, and 1.4 Å, respectively [32]. To investigate the structural stability, the calculated C-F bond lengths and cohesive energies of all the relaxed F-doped CD structures are shown in Fig. 2 .

From Fig. 2, it was found that the C-F bond lengths in edge-CF-1 and edge-CF-2 structures are 1.337 Å and 1.336 Å, respectively, which are in agreement with the previously theoretical value 1.35 Å calculated by Abdelati et al. [61]. In the two cases, the C-F bonds are slightly shorter than typical covalent bonds, indicating that the C-F bonds of edge CF groups belong to the covalent bonding type. The C-F bond lengths of edge-CF2–1 and edge-CF2–2 structures are 1.396 Å and 1.409 Å, respectively, which are also consistent with the typical covalent bond length. The C-F bond lengths of edge-CF3–1, 2 structures are about 1.34 Å, suggesting that these C-F bonds are also covalent. However, for six basal plane CF groups, the C-F bond lengths significantly change from 1.423 Å for basal plane-CF-2 to 1.572 Å for basal plane-CF-5, indicating that the bonding types vary from covalent to semi-ionic bonding, and similar results also have been observed by Zhou et al. [62]. In this bonding state, F will deprive the electronic charge of carbon, introducing p-doping character in the CDs. This great variation in the bond lengths of basal plane CF groups indicates that the electronic structure is closely related to the carbon network environment [49]. In a word, the F-doped configurations lead to the various C-F bond lengths and types, which will affect the stability and fluorescence properties of the CDs.

Fig. 2 also summarizes the cohesive energies of all structures to assess the structural stability. It is clear that the cohesive energies of all structures are negative, indicating that F-doped structures are stable and cannot spontaneously break up into individual atoms. The cohesive energy of circumcoronene is −7.6001 eV. Compared with circumcoronene, the cohesive energies of CF and CF2 groups with edge or basal plane types are lower, whereas those of CF3 groups are higher. This result indicates that single and double F atoms bonded with one C atom can improve the structural stability of CDs, but the simultaneous tri-doping of CF3 groups at one site will destroy the stability of carbon networks. Among all the structures, the edge-CF-1 and edge-CF-2 structures exhibit the lowest cohesive energy, suggesting that a single F atom bonded with an edge C atom will have the most significant contribution to the structural stability of CDs.

Moreover, analyzing the effects of F-doping sites on cohesive energy, one can see that the energy differences between edge-CF-1 and edge-CF-2, edge-CF2–1 and edge-CF2–2, edge-CF3–1 and edge-CF3–2 structures are 0.0002 eV, 0.0055 eV and 0.0045 eV, respectively. Also, the maximum cohesive energy difference in six basal plane CF structures is only 0.0109 eV. Usually, when the atomic bonding type is the same, the shorter the bond length, the higher the bond energy, and the more stable the structure. Except for the CF3 groups, the edge CF groups have the shortest covalent C-F bonds and exhibit the most stable structures. For the CF2 groups, the covalent C-F bond lengths are slightly lengthened by 0.05A, and the structure stability is correspondingly reduced. The basal plane CF groups have the longest C-F semi-ionic bonds, and thus the stability of these structures is further reduced.

Experimentally, the F-doping pattern in CDs can be detected by splitting the peaks of the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and the higher the peak intensity, the higher the corresponding functional group content. Recently, Xu et al. observed that the content of CF and CF2 groups is greater than that of CF3 group in the synthesis of CDs [49]. In this work, the calculated results of cohesive energy clearly tell that the content of edge CF groups is greater than that of basal plane CF groups, and the content of CF3 groups is the lowest in CDs. The CF3 groups have no positive contribution to the structural stability, and thus these F-doping configurations are difficult to form during the synthesis of CDs. The present results theoretically explain the rationality of various F-doped structures and further reveal the most possible doped types and configurations from a thermodynamic perspective.

The electronic properties and fluorescence emission spectra of all structures were calculated, and Table 1 shows fluorescence emission wavelengths, oscillator strengths, and charge transfer values between F atoms and carbon cores. Comparing the emission wavelengths of all structures, one can see that F-doping causes a redshift in the fluorescence emission. According to the degree of spectral redshift, these structures can be classified into two categories. The first category including the edge CF and CF3 groups only exhibits a slight redshift, while the second category shows an obvious emission redshift of about 95 nm ∼ 198 nm, including CF2 and basal plane CF groups. Based on the analysis of the conjugation properties, those structures maintaining original conjugation properties only produce a slight degree of emission redshift, and once the conjugation system is broken, that is, the electronic state from sp 2 to sp 3 hybridized state, a significant fluorescence emission redshift will be caused. The result suggests that there is a close relationship between the fluorescence emission redshift and the conjugation properties of the CDs. In the cases of edge CF and CF3 groups, one or three F atoms bonded to one C atom have almost no impact on the initial conjugation property of the sp 2 carbon domain. However, for the CF2 and basal plane CF groups, the introduction of F partially disrupts the intact conjugated π domains of the carbon networks and directly affects the π-π* electron transition process, leading to a remarkable fluorescence emission redshift.

Recently, Gotsu et al. attempted to introduce F atoms into dibenzo anthracene and benzopyrene, and found that the fluorescence emission spectra of these two organic molecules were redshifted by 8 nm and 13 nm, respectively [63]. However, Ding et al. also investigated the effects of F-doping and they found a quite different result that the solid-state F-doped CDs remarkably cause fluorescence emission redshift by 172 nm [40]. In fact, the seemingly contradictory experimental results suggest that F atoms possibly enter into different doped sites in CDs. To better explain these experimental phenomena and explore the effect of F-doped configurations, two CF2 groups and six basal plane CF groups induced remarkable emission redshift were selected to calculate their fluorescence emission spectra without considering a solvent effect, and the results are given in Table S1 in the Supporting Information. The present calculations show that the fluorescence emission redshift of these eight structures in the vacuum can range from 125 nm to 249 nm. Among all the cases, the emission redshift of basal plane-CF-6 is 171 nm, which is very close to the experimental value measured by Ding et al. [40]. This result reveals that when F atoms are introduced into the solid CDs, these F atoms most likely exist in a basal plane type and the formed C-F bonds are away from the edge of carbon networks. The present results explain the seemingly contradictory experimental phenomena and give a better theoretical prediction for the F-doped sites, which usually cannot be accurately detected experimentally.

The greater oscillator intensity between the ground state and the excited state means that it is easier to occur the electron transition, and therefore the corresponding emission peaks in the fluorescence spectrum will be stronger [64,65]. From Table 1, it can be seen that the oscillator strength of the fluorescence emission for circumcoronene is zero due to its high symmetry [63,66]. However, the introduction of the F atom breaks the initial symmetry of circumcoronene, so that the fluorescence emission oscillator strength no longer equals zero in F-doped CD structures. Especially, the F-doping in the CF2 and basal plane CF groups significantly reduces the symmetry of circumcoronene, and thus their oscillator strengths are relatively higher.

As we all know, the electron transition of circumcoronene belongs to local excitation [67]. Therefore, investigating the charge transfer values q between F atoms and the rest part of CDs is necessary to analyze the properties of the electron transition during the fluorescence emission of F-doped CDs. The larger the q value, the larger the component of charge transfer transition, whereas the closer the q value is to zero, the larger the component of local excitation. The charge transfer values of edge CF and CF3 groups are very close to zero, which suggests the fluorescence emission of these structures was determinedly induced by local excitation, leading to a slight redshift. In contrast, the charge transfer values of CF2 and basal plane CF groups are relatively large, which suggests that the electron transition mainly attributed to charge transfer transition, and therefore a significant redshift in fluorescence emission is produced.

The energy levels of frontier molecular orbitals of circumcoronene and F-doped structures are depicted in Fig. 3 . The frontier molecular orbitals consist of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). The band gap value of the pristine circumcoronene is 5.47 eV, while those of all F-doped structures are less than this value, suggesting that the introduction of F atoms can reduce the band gaps of the CDs. For the edge CF and CF3 groups, both HOMO and LUMO levels slightly decrease, but the LUMO levels decrease a bit more than HOMO levels, resulting in a slight reduction of their band gaps. For the CF2 and basal plane CF groups, the HOMO levels rise more significantly than the LUMO levels, leading to decreased gaps. The asymmetric stabilization of the frontier molecular orbitals causes the band gap to decrease [58].

According to the degree of band gap reduction, the F-doped CDs also can be classified into two categories. The edge CF and CF3 groups, which have a smaller reduction in the band gap, belong to the first category. The second category includes the CF2 and basal plane CF groups, which exhibit a greater reduction in the band gap. Combined with the analysis of fluorescence characteristics in Table 1, one can see that the classification of band gap is consistent with that of the degree of fluorescence emission redshift. In molecular orbital theory, the HOMO and LUMO levels of circumcoronene represent π and π* orbitals, respectively, and the σ bond only has a minimal effect on the fluorescence properties of CDs. However, the introduction of F atoms makes n orbitals formed by their lone electron pairs contribute to the electron transitions and thus lead to the n-π* component during the electron transition. To further analyze the n-π* component and understand the fluorescence emission properties of circumcoronene and F-doped CDs, NTOs were analyzed for the electronic transition processes from the first excited state S1 to the ground state S0 [68], and topological surface maps of the NTOs have been plotted as shown in Fig. 4 .

From Fig. 4, it can be found that for the original circumcoronene, the highest occupied natural transition orbital (HONTO) and the lowest unoccupied natural transition orbital (LUNTO) are delocalized throughout the whole structure during the electron transition. Compared with circumcoronene, the HONTOs and LUNTOs of edge CF and CF3 groups are almost unchanged, but 2p orbitals of F atoms in these structures are involved in the electron transition process, extending π orbitals. This is equivalent to the extension of the sp 2 domains, which is consistent with a slight redshift of the fluorescence emission. The electron transitions of edge CF and CF3 groups consist of two parts, namely π-π* transition and n-π* transition. The π-π* transitions present that the π electrons of C = C double bonds transfer to the corresponding π* orbitals, and the n-π* transition presents the n electrons from the lone electron pairs of F atom to the π* orbitals of C = C double bonds. For the CF2 and basal plane CF groups, the introduction of F atoms has a remarkable effect on HONTOs but not on LUNTOs, which is consistent with the change of the HOMO and LUMO energy levels in Fig. 3. The electrons of HONTOs are redistributed near F atoms, and therefore the contribution of F 2p orbitals becomes more remarkable in the electron transition process. At the same time, due to the localization of HONTOs near F atoms, more obvious charge transfer transitions and a larger degree of fluorescence emission redshift occur.

The CDD can further describe the charge transfer in electron transitions and explain the fluorescence mechanism [58]. The CDD diagrams for all the F-doped CD structures are shown in Fig. 5 . Here, the CDD diagrams represent the charge density difference between the ground state S0 and the excited state S1, and blue regions indicate the decrease in charge density of S1 relative to S0, and the green regions indicate the increase in charge density of S1 to S0. In other words, it can be regarded as the hole distributed in the blue region and the electron distributed in the green region. The CDD diagram of circumcoronene (shown in Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information) reveals that the electron-hole pairs are delocalized throughout the entire structure, suggesting that this electron transition belongs to local excitation. Compared with circumcoronene, the F-doping of edge CF and CF3 groups slightly changes the distribution of the charge density difference, but it cannot fundamentally change the essence of local excitation. For CF2 and basal plane CF groups, the electrons and holes are almost separated into two independent regions, and the holes are mainly concentrated near F atoms. In contrast, the electrons are delocalized in the whole structure. The significant electron-hole separation in these types of F-doping, particularly in the CF2 and basal plane CF groups, leads to electron transitions that are predominantly caused by charge transfer transitions. Finally, it will lead to a significant redshift in the fluorescence emission process.

According to the CDD diagrams, the transfer of electrons in the fluorescence emission process can be revealed. The results show that for edge CF and CF3 groups, the holes and electrons are delocalized over the whole carbon domains, hence the electron only transfers between adjacent positions, and therefore this local excitation mechanism induces weak fluorescence emission redshift. For CF2 and basal plane CF groups, the electron-hole separation is obvious, the charge transfer transition is trigged and herein the fluorescence emission is remarkably redshifted.

In this work, the effects of F-doping on the structural stability and fluorescence emission of CDs were systematically investigated using DFT and TDDFT. Various F-doped types and configurations including the edge CF group, CF2 group, CF3 group, and basal plane CF groups were constructed to investigate the optical properties of F-doped CDs.

Firstly, the C-F bond lengths and cohesive energies of the ground state F-doped CD structures were analyzed and the results show that the edge CF groups can enhance the structural stability of CDs. Then, the fluorescence emission as well as electron transition information of all structures were calculated. For these F-doped structures, the types and configurations of F-doping result in the reduction of the band gap to varying degrees. The fluorescence emission of the edge CF and CF3 groups slightly redshifted, due to weaker vibrational intensity and less charge transfer between F atoms and carbon core during the electron transition. In contrast, the fluorescence emission of the CF2 and basal plane CF groups significantly redshifted between 125 nm and 249 nm, suggesting that the charge transfer transition is triggered in these cases. These results predict the types and configurations of F-doping in CDs from a thermodynamic perspective and reveal the fluorescence emission mechanism and the behavior of electrons of F-doped CDs.

Up to now, the synthesis of long-wavelength CDs remains challenging. The doping of F atoms is an effective pathway to promote the research work of the red and near-infrared CDs. Due to the limited experimental measurements, DFT and TDDFT theoretical calculations were necessary to give a clear description about the relationship between the atomic structure and fluorescence emission of the CDs. The present work provides a deeper understanding of the optical properties of F-doped CDs and is helpful in designing and synthesizing CDs with near-infrared fluorescence emission by modulating the site and configuration of F-doping. In future, the F-doped CDs with near-infrared fluorescence emission have the potential applications in optoelectronic devices, biomedical imaging, drug delivery, etc.

Jianen Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Li Guan: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration. Tao Luo: Visualization, Supervision. Tao Yin: Supervision, Formal analysis. Weicai Shi: Visualization, Supervision, Methodology. Xiaojie Ren: Visualization, Supervision. Youshi Lan: Supervision, Software. Fenghe Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Xu Li: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62075055, No. 62175075, No. 61974009), the Central Project Guide Local Science and Technology for Development of Hebei Province (No. 226Z1103G), and the Interdisciplinary Research Project of Hebei University (No. DXK202012). We also appreciate the High Performance Supercomputer Center of Hebei University.
